title: Remove the dead 'snapshot' render mode from PtySession (pyte machinery is unreachable)
description: PtySession carries a full server-side pyte screen-reconstruction mode that no backend can ever activate. `render_mode` defaults to "raw" (pty_session.py:211) and the only backend that sets it explicitly hardcodes "raw" (claude_code.py:222, with a comment explaining snapshot mode was abandoned because it garbled Claude Code's TUI). `_snapshot_enabled()` (pty_session.py:285-286) is therefore always False, making all of this unreachable: the pyte import + fallback (51-54), SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL_SEC / SNAPSHOT_POST_RESIZE_DELAY_SEC tunables (68-80), fields `_screen/_screen_stream/_snapshot_task/_snapshot_dirty` (257-260), `_init_screen_renderer` (693-714), the snapshot branches in `_record_output` (718-724), `_handle_output` (728-734), `wait_until_input_ready` (828-834), `attach` (1094), `resize` (1189-1208), `close` (1337-1339), plus `_schedule_snapshot_broadcast` (849-864), `current_snapshot` (866-875, never called anywhere â€” verified by grep), and `_snapshot_bytes` (877-902). Greps confirm no test, frontend, or api code references snapshot mode or render_mode. Every reader of this 1534-line file currently has to track two rendering modes when only one exists.
sketch: Delete: pyte import block, SNAPSHOT_* constants, the four snapshot fields, `_snapshot_enabled`, `_init_screen_renderer`, `_record_output`'s pyte branch (inline `_append_scrollback` at its two call sites), the snapshot early-return in `_handle_output`, the snapshot branch in `wait_until_input_ready`, `_schedule_snapshot_broadcast`, `current_snapshot`, `_snapshot_bytes`, the snapshot block in `resize`, and the `_snapshot_task` cancellation in `close`. In `attach`, `snapshot` is then always b"" â€” drop the variable and the `if snapshot:` replay (the tmux `request_repaint()` already handles screen restore). Keep the `render_mode` field + `recipe.get("render_mode", "raw")` plumbing and the `status()["render_mode"]` key as inert pass-throughs if wire-format paranoia is desired, or remove them too since nothing consumes them. Also drop `"render_mode": "raw"` from claude_code.py:222 and its 8-line comment.
impact: ~170 lines removed from the largest module in scope; _handle_output / attach / resize / wait_until_input_ready each lose a whole conditional dimension and become single-path
guidance: Corrections/additions to the sketch, all verified: (1) Do NOT remove pyte from runner-service/requirements.txt:44 â€” backends/claude_interactive.py:49 hard-imports pyte and uses it extensively; only pty_session.py's guarded try/except import is dead. (2) Also delete the _init_screen_renderer() call at pty_session.py:455 in start(), which the sketch omits. (3) _broadcast (pty_session.py:1049-1063) becomes dead after this removal â€” its sole caller is line 862 inside the snapshot scheduler; delete it too, but KEEP _evict_client and _kick_client (used by _handle_output's dead-client handling at 754-766). (4) The sketch says inline _append_scrollback at _record_output's "two call sites", but one (731) is inside the deleted snapshot branch â€” only one caller (744) survives; inline or keep the one-line wrapper, either is fine. (5) Removing the render_mode field, the recipe.get at 1499, claude_code.py:222, and the status() key at 1238 together is safe: grep confirms nothing (api, frontend, tests) consumes them; if wire paranoia is preferred, emitting a literal "render_mode": "raw" in status() is the conservative fallback. (6) Update stale snapshot-referencing prose: wait_until_input_ready docstring line 819 ("in snapshot mode the live pyte screen is consulted directly"), attach comments at 1091-1093/1110-1112, and condense claude_code.py's 8-line comment (214-221) â€” a one-line "server-side snapshot rendering was abandoned (garbled Claude Code's TUI); xterm.js renders raw bytes" preserves the useful history. Leave the historical snapshot mentions in frontend TerminalTab.tsx comments alone (out of scope). (7) Verification note: runner-service tests skip on Windows (test_pty_session.py:9-10 skips on nt) â€” run pytest in the container/CI to exercise the module import.

title: Extract the triplicated auth-bootstrap and spawn-prep sequences in ClaudeCodeBackend
description: claude_code.py duplicates two large blocks three times. (1) Token bootstrap/refresh/guard: run_sync lines 545-601, stream_events lines 880-947, and a trimmed copy in prepare_interactive lines 128-137 â€” ~60 lines each of 'bootstrap from global token if owner record empty â†’ proactive refresh if expired â†’ final no-token guard â†’ build login_url + auth_required payload', identical except run_sync returns a dict while stream_events yields an event. (2) Spawn-prep: `_resolve_effective_user â†’ configure_claude_mcp â†’ configure_claude_instructions â†’ _apply_permissions_to_settings â†’ seed_onboarding_state â†’ seed_credentials_file` repeated verbatim at 139-144, 608-623, and 950-956. Also (3) try_exchange_code_from_prompt handling is duplicated at 532-543 vs 866-878. Any auth fix currently has to be applied in two or three places (the reliability campaign's commit history shows exactly this pattern).
sketch:
Add two private helpers:

async def _ensure_auth(self, agent_user, agent_id) -> Optional[dict]:
    """Returns None when a usable token exists; otherwise an
    {'status':'auth_required','error':...,'login_url':...} payload."""
    # body = current 545-601 logic, covering both agent_user and global paths

async def _prepare_spawn(self, agent_id, agent_user) -> Optional[dict]:  # returns effective_user
    effective_user = self._resolve_effective_user(agent_id, agent_user)
    await run_blocking(configure_claude_mcp, effective_user, agent_id)
    await run_blocking(configure_claude_instructions, effective_user, agent_id)
    self._apply_permissions_to_settings(effective_user, self._get_permissions(agent_id))
    seed_onboarding_state(effective_user)
    await run_blocking(seed_credentials_file, effective_user)
    return effective_user

run_sync: `gate = await self._ensure_auth(...); if gate: return gate`. stream_events: `if gate: yield {'type':'error','content':gate['error'],'login_url':gate['login_url']}; return`. prepare_interactive reuses both.
impact: ~120 duplicated lines collapse to ~60 shared; run_sync shrinks from ~330 to ~230 lines and stream_events from ~210 to ~140; auth fixes become single-site
guidance: Two corrections. (1) The sketch's claim that 'prepare_interactive reuses both' is wrong for _ensure_auth: the copy at lines 128-137 is deliberately bootstrap-only (comment at 125-127: the CLI refreshes itself in the TUI), and prepare_interactive returns a PTY recipe dict with no channel for an auth_required gate. Decompose three-tier instead: _bootstrap_owner_token(agent_user) (the global-token seed, shared by all three; note prepare_interactive's copy lacks the two logger.info lines â€” unifying adds them there, harmless), _ensure_auth = bootstrap + proactive refresh + final guard (run_sync/stream_events only; must also cover the agent_user-is-None global path at 582-601/924-947 and fetch get_token_cooldown_until() itself), and _prepare_spawn as sketched (move run_sync's rationale comments at 611-622 into the helper). (2) Behavior-preservation traps: (a) keep gate payload shapes exact â€” run_sync returns {'status':'auth_required','output':'','error':...,'login_url':...} while the exchange-failure return at 539-543 has NO login_url key; stream yields {'type':'error','content':...,'login_url':...}; do not add keys where a path currently omits them. (b) Logging diverges: stream_events logs '[Auth] No valid token for {who}' and 'continuing with existing token' where run_sync is silent â€” unify on the richer stream_events logging (log text isn't contract, but make it a conscious choice). (c) The dead else-branch at 901-904 disappears in the helper â€” behavior-preserving, call it out in the commit message. (d) Skip or defer part (3) (try_exchange_code_from_prompt dedup): after dict-vs-yield adaptation the shared core is ~3 lines, not worth an abstraction. (e) No tests cover any of these paths, so verify by line-diffing the extracted helper against both originals rather than relying on pytest.

title: Template prepare_interactive in CliBackend and share the model-spec resolver (per-CLI config plumbing, quantified)
description: All five CLI backends re-implement the same prepare_interactive skeleton: ensure_agent_user â†’ _resolve_effective_user â†’ run_blocking(_configure_mcp) â†’ run_blocking(_configure_instructions) â†’ run_blocking(_agent_env) â†’ build argv â†’ get_subprocess_kwargs â†’ return {cmd,cwd,env,preexec_fn,...}. Copies: opencode.py:418-450 (33 lines), openclaw.py:234-268 (35), hermes.py:161-197 (37), aider.py:142-163 (22), codex.py:126-209 (84). ~12-15 of those lines per backend are byte-identical plumbing (~70 duplicated lines total). Separately, the `--model` resolvers are 4 copies of one function: `_resolve_opencode_model` (opencode.py:66-83) and `_resolve_aider_model` (aider.py:72-89) are identical 18-line bodies differing only in the providerâ†’namespace dict; `_resolve_codex_model` (codex.py:82-96) and `_resolve_openclaw_model` (openclaw.py:47-57) are the degenerate map-less form. Today a fix to the spawn-prep ordering (e.g. 'warm LLM cache before building argv') must be replicated 5 times.
sketch:
In CliBackend:

async def prepare_interactive(self, agent_id, owner_id=None) -> dict:
    await self._pre_interactive(agent_id, owner_id)            # hook, default no-op
    agent_user = await ensure_agent_user(agent_id, owner_id=owner_id) if agent_id else None
    effective_user = self._resolve_effective_user(agent_id, agent_user)
    await run_blocking(self._configure_mcp, effective_user, agent_id)
    await run_blocking(self._configure_instructions, effective_user, agent_id)
    env = await run_blocking(self._agent_env, effective_user, agent_id)
    cmd = self._interactive_cmd(agent_id, self._get_permissions(agent_id))   # abstract hook
    self._verify_model_config(agent_id, model=self._cli_model(agent_id), env=env)
    kwargs = get_subprocess_kwargs(effective_user) or {}
    recipe = {"cmd": cmd, "cwd": self._resolve_cwd(agent_id), "env": env,
              "preexec_fn": kwargs.get("preexec_fn")}
    recipe.update(self._interactive_extras(agent_id, owner_id, agent_user, effective_user))  # hook, default {}
    return recipe

Hooks per backend: opencode `_pre_interactive` calls `_ensure_config_dir`; codex's calls `_hydrate_for_exec`; hermes `_interactive_extras` returns the files_watch_paths/files_on_change pair; codex's returns the creds_watch triple. Shared resolver in cli_backend.py:

def resolve_model_spec(llm_config: Optional[dict], provider_map: Optional[dict] = None) -> str

with opencode/aider passing their maps and codex/openclaw passing None.
impact: ~100 lines removed across 5 backends; each per-CLI file becomes mostly declarative (argv + extras), and spawn-prep ordering bugs become fixable in one place
guidance: Corrections required for behavior preservation: (1) EXCLUDE codex from the prepare_interactive template, or give it env/verify opt-out hooks â€” codex.py:164 never calls _agent_env (the `hasattr(self, "_build_env")` branch is dead; _build_env exists nowhere), it uses sanitize_env(os.environ, agent_user) with agent_user not effective_user; the template's _agent_env step would inject OPENAI_API_KEY/GITHUB_TOKEN into codex's interactive spawn and can change codex auth selection. Limit codex changes to: share the resolver, replace the inline cwd duplication (codex.py:145-148) with self._resolve_cwd, and delete the vestigial hasattr guards (codex.py:137-138, 164-168). (2) Move the _pre_interactive hook AFTER ensure_agent_user/_resolve_effective_user and pass (agent_user, effective_user) â€” opencode's _ensure_config_dir (opencode.py:307-337) needs agent HOME, which doesn't exist yet on a first spawn if the hook runs before ensure_agent_user (would resurrect the EACCES bug it fixes). Note it's also currently called synchronously, not via run_blocking â€” keep that. (3) Make _verify_model_config a skippable hook: hermes (deliberately, see hermes.py:14-27) and codex do not call it today; unconditional verify emits a misleading 'model did NOT resolve to a CLI model argument' warning for hermes' by-design terminal-driven model. (4) Add a post-configure hook slot for openclaw's configure_openclaw_permissions(effective_user or agent_user, ...) (openclaw.py:241-245) â€” do NOT fold it into _configure_mcp, or the headless run_sync/stream_events paths start executing it (and _build_command at openclaw.py:281 already calls it with None â†’ double writes with different user args). (5) resolve_model_spec must branch explicitly on provider_map is None â†’ return bare model; the natural map.get(provider, provider) fallback would change codex/openclaw argv from `--model o3` to `--model openai/o3`. (6) Keep _resolve_opencode_model/_resolve_codex_model/_resolve_openclaw_model as thin module-level wrappers â€” tests import them by name (test_opencode.py:11, test_codex.py:11, test_local_model_config.py:76). (7) prepare_interactive has zero pytest coverage; add recipe-shape tests (cmd/cwd/env keys, hermes files_watch_paths, codex creds_watch_path) before refactoring, since the 46-test suite gives no safety net on this path. Benign-by-inspection: aider lacking a _configure_mcp override (base no-op is harmless via run_blocking), and hermes/openclaw building cmd before env (order-independent).

title: Dedupe the owner/agent OAuth flow pairs in claude_oauth.py
description: Three near-identical pairs: (1) `initiate_agent_login` (169-193) and `initiate_owner_login` (206-230) are byte-for-byte identical 25-line bodies except the flow dict and log label â€” and both re-implement what `_build_auth_url` (64-81) already does, with a third URL-construction style (f-string vs urlencode-by-hand). (2) `try_exchange_code_from_prompt` (243-359) contains two ~48-line branches (owner: 260-304, agent: 306-353) that differ only in which flow store they read, which save function they call, and log prefixes â€” every error message string is duplicated. A wording fix or a new error case must be applied twice, and the function is 117 lines for what is one algorithm.
sketch:
def _initiate_login(flows: dict, key: str, label: str) -> str:
    if key in flows: return flows[key]["auth_url"]
    verifier, challenge = _generate_pkce()
    state = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    flows[key] = {"code_verifier": verifier, "state": state, "auth_url": _authorize_url(challenge, state)}
    ...

initiate_agent_login = lambda agent_id: _initiate_login(_agent_oauth_flows, agent_id, "Agent")  # (as thin defs)

async def _exchange_pending(flows, key, auth_code, state, persist: Callable[[dict], Awaitable[bool] | bool], label) -> dict:
    # current 260-304 body, parametrized; persist closure does save_owner_token(...) or ensure_agent_user+save_agent_token(...)

try_exchange_code_from_prompt keeps its gating (code#state match) and dispatches to _exchange_pending twice. Public signatures unchanged.
impact: ~90 lines removed (file shrinks 360 â†’ ~270); the exchange algorithm exists once instead of 2.5 times
guidance: Corrections/traps: (1) Do NOT fold the per-flow URL into _build_auth_url despite the description's claim that they "re-implement" it â€” _build_auth_url (64-81) adds code=true as first param and percent-encodes redirect_uri/scope, while the per-flow f-string (177-186) omits code=true, leaves redirect_uri unencoded, and encodes scope spaces as '+'; _build_auth_url also mutates globals _oauth_code_verifier/_oauth_state, which would corrupt the global flow. Add a new private helper reproducing the f-string at 177-186 byte-for-byte (the sketch's _authorize_url is correct; the description's framing is not). (2) _generate_pkce() is output-equivalent to the inline PKCE at 172-175 (encode("ascii") vs encode() â€” same for ASCII token_urlsafe input), safe to reuse. (3) Preserve log strings exactly: agent ids are truncated [:12] only in the exchange branches (lines 317, 319, 331, 342, 348) and in the token_http_request context string, NOT in initiate_agent_login (192 logs the full id). Pass label and display_id as separate params. (4) The persist closure must execute inside the helper's try block so an ensure_agent_user exception still hits pop+generic-error (350-353); make both persist closures async (wrap the sync save_owner_token) rather than mixing sync/async returns. Keep the agent semantics: persisted=False when ensure_agent_user returns falsy, no raise. (5) Preserve ordering: persist BEFORE pop on success (290â†’291 and 336-339â†’340); pop BEFORE return on every error path. (6) Keep the gating (key in flows AND state matches flow["state"]) in try_exchange_code_from_prompt itself, with dispatch order owner â†’ agent â†’ global fallback (355-357), falling through on state mismatch. (7) Keep the public initiates as plain defs, not lambdas, since claude_code.py imports them by name. (8) No test coverage exists for this module â€” diffs must stay mechanical since there is no safety net.

title: Dedupe owner/agent twin functions: refresh_*_token and *_auth_callback
description: Two more owner/agent mirror pairs. (1) `refresh_owner_token` (claude_token_store.py:765-795) and `refresh_agent_token` (798-830) share an identical 30-line skeleton: get refresh token â†’ build payload â†’ token_http_request â†’ handle _already_valid/_invalid_grant (invalidate + 60s cooldown) â†’ extract access_token â†’ persist â†’ log; they differ only in the getter/invalidator/saver/log-prefix (and refresh_agent_token already delegates to refresh_owner_token when owner_id is set). (2) `agent_auth_callback` (claude_code.py:1122-1148) and `owner_auth_callback` (1175-1197) build the same grant payload, call token_http_request, validate access_token, persist, and pop the flow â€” ~25 duplicated lines differing in flow getters and the save call. The _invalid_grant/cooldown logic being triplicated (also in refresh_oauth_token, 724-762) means cooldown bugfixes need three edits.
sketch:
claude_token_store.py:

async def _refresh_with(refresh_token, expiry_probe, invalidate, persist, label) -> bool:
    # one copy of the 765-830 skeleton; persist returns bool

async def refresh_owner_token(owner_id):
    return await _refresh_with(
        await run_blocking(get_owner_refresh_token, owner_id),
        {"_owner_id": owner_id},
        lambda: run_blocking(invalidate_owner_token, owner_id),
        lambda at, rt, exp: run_blocking(save_owner_token, owner_id, at, refresh_token=rt, expires_in=exp),
        f"owner {owner_id}")

claude_code.py:

async def _oauth_callback(self, flow, code, persist: Callable[[str, str, int], bool], pop) -> dict:
    # one copy of the exchange/validate/persist/pop body

agent_auth_callback / owner_auth_callback become ~8-line wrappers.
impact: ~55 duplicated lines collapse to ~30; the invalid-grant/cooldown policy lives in one function
guidance: Corrections and traps for the implementer: (a) Do NOT fold refresh_oauth_token (claude_token_store.py:724-762) into the new _refresh_with helper even though the description notes the cooldown logic is triplicated there â€” it differs materially: whole-body try/except returning False (owner/agent versions propagate exceptions), `result.get("refresh_token") or refresh_token` instead of `.get(key, default)`, save_token called without checking a return value, and separate None/_already_valid checks. Fusing it would change exception and empty-string-refresh-token behavior. Keep the helper scoped to the owner/agent pair as sketched. (b) The synthetic `{"_owner_id": owner_id}` dict passed as agent_user is load-bearing: token_http_request:650-651 branches on `_owner_id` for the under-lock _already_valid re-probe; thread it through verbatim. (c) _refresh_with must live in claude_token_store.py and declare `global _token_cooldown_until` so the cooldown write hits the module global (also read via get_token_cooldown_until at :144). (d) Owner-side callables are `run_blocking(...)` coroutines while agent-side getter/invalidator/saver are plain sync calls; either await uniformly via small async shims (an awaited no-suspension coroutine does not change event-loop ordering) or accept may-be-awaitable callables â€” do not switch the agent calls to run_blocking/to_thread, which would add a thread hop. (e) Preserve exact `return bool(result)` semantics for the not-result/_already_valid short-circuit (empty dict returns False with no further logging). (f) In claude_code.py the sketch under-specifies _oauth_callback: the success responses differ (agent_id vs owner_id key, different messages), so the helper needs a success-payload (or id-key+message) parameter in addition to persist/pop; keep the agent wrapper's ensure_agent_user step between the flow check and the helper call to preserve error ordering. (g) codex.py:513/563 defines same-named callbacks but on a completely different blob/exchange mechanism â€” out of scope; do not attempt cross-backend unification.

title: Dead-code sweep: codex.prepare_interactive's phantom _build_env path, unused _reconcile_json_mcp, unused config imports
description: Three confirmed-dead artifacts that actively mislead readers. (1) codex.py:164-168: `env = self._build_env(agent_user) if hasattr(self, "_build_env") else None` â€” `_build_env` does not exist anywhere in the repo (verified by grep), so the 'fallback' sanitize_env path is the ONLY path; a reader must hunt for a method that was never written. Same function: line 130 imports `CliBackend as _CliBackend` and never uses it; lines 137-138 guard `_resolve_effective_user` with hasattr although CliBackend always defines it; lines 145-148 re-implement `self._resolve_cwd(agent_id)` inline with two local imports; line 170 re-imports get_subprocess_kwargs under an alias. (2) runner_mcp_config.py:299-341: `_reconcile_json_mcp` (43 lines) has zero callers â€” every JSON writer grew its own sidecar-based variant instead. (3) claude_interactive.py:54-56 imports CLAUDE_FALLBACK_LLM_URL/KEY/MODEL from config but never uses them (the resolver moved to fallback_llm_resolver.py; only a docstring mentions them).
sketch: codex.prepare_interactive: replace 164-168 with `env = await run_blocking(self._agent_env, effective_user, agent_id)` IF the LLM/GitHub env injection is wanted (behavior change â€” needs the owner's call), or minimally with the current actual behavior `env = sanitize_env(os.environ, agent_user)` plus a one-line comment; drop the `_CliBackend` import, the two hasattr guards, and replace 145-148 with `cwd = self._resolve_cwd(agent_id)`; hoist the get_subprocess_kwargs import to the top. Delete `_reconcile_json_mcp` wholesale. Delete the three unused config imports in claude_interactive.py.
impact: ~70 lines removed; codex.prepare_interactive stops implying capabilities (env builders, optional mixins) that don't exist
guidance: Take ONLY the behavior-preserving option for the env line: `env = sanitize_env(os.environ, agent_user)`. Do NOT switch to `run_blocking(self._agent_env, effective_user, agent_id)` in this refactor â€” _agent_env (cli_backend.py:252-330) injects provider API keys (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY/OPENAI_API_KEY/etc.) and base-URL overrides from the agent's LLM config into the spawned TUI's environment, a real behavior change; it would also swap agent_user for effective_user. Keep `agent_user` (not `effective_user`) as the sanitize_env argument â€” that is what the current fallback passes even when runAsRoot nulls effective_user. Hoisting imports to the top of codex.py is cycle-safe: command_security imports only re/os/typing/config.logger, and claude_token_store is already a top-level import (codex.py:71). When deleting _reconcile_json_mcp, keep the _MANAGED_KEY constant and _pulsarMcpUpdatedAt writes elsewhere â€” they are used by live functions at runner_mcp_config.py:139-170 and 551-560. Run pytest (46 tests) after; nothing in tests touches these paths.

title: Replace the providerâ†’env-var elif ladder in CliBackend._agent_env with lookup tables
description: cli_backend.py:262-314 wires the per-agent API key and endpoint into env vars via a 13-branch elif chain for the key (`anthropicâ†’ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, â€¦ `ollamaâ†’OLLAMA_API_KEY`) followed by a second 3-branch chain for the endpoint, with the OPENAI_COMPATIBLE_LOCAL_PROVIDERS tuple consulted twice. Adding a provider today means finding the right elif slot in a 50-line block; the google/gemini case (two env vars) and the ollama case (endpoint also synthesizes OPENAI_BASE_URL + placeholder key) are buried mid-chain. The same provider-name aliasing knowledge is restated as dicts in opencode.py:46-63 and aider.py:48-69, so this module is the only one expressing it as control flow instead of data.
sketch:
Module-level table:

_PROVIDER_KEY_ENV: dict[str, tuple[str, ...]] = {
    "anthropic": ("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",), "claude": ("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",), "claude-paid": ("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",),
    "openai": ("OPENAI_API_KEY",), "openrouter": ("OPENROUTER_API_KEY",), "mistral": ("MISTRAL_API_KEY",),
    "google": ("GOOGLE_API_KEY", "GEMINI_API_KEY"), "gemini": ("GOOGLE_API_KEY", "GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    "groq": ("GROQ_API_KEY",), "deepseek": ("DEEPSEEK_API_KEY",), "xai": ("XAI_API_KEY",), "grok": ("XAI_API_KEY",),
    "nvidia": ("NVIDIA_API_KEY",), "huggingface": ("HF_TOKEN",), "ollama": ("OLLAMA_API_KEY",),
    **{p: ("OPENAI_API_KEY",) for p in OPENAI_COMPATIBLE_LOCAL_PROVIDERS},
}

for var in _PROVIDER_KEY_ENV.get(provider, ()): env[var] = api_key

Keep the endpoint handling as three small named helpers (`_apply_openai_endpoint`, `_apply_anthropic_endpoint`, `_apply_ollama_endpoint`) dispatched from a second small map, preserving the existing comments inside each helper.
impact: ~52 lines â†’ ~28, and 'which env var does provider X get' becomes a one-glance table consistent with the sibling backends' dict style
guidance: 1) Endpoint aliasing is asymmetric: the key chain treats claude/claude-paid as anthropic, but the endpoint branch at cli_backend.py:302 matches only provider == "anthropic" â€” a "claude" provider with an endpoint gets no ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL today. The endpoint dispatch must key on exactly {"openai", *OPENAI_COMPATIBLE_LOCAL_PROVIDERS, "anthropic", "ollama"}; do not normalize the claude aliases in. 2) Keep key application BEFORE endpoint handling: line 300's `if not env.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")` sk-local placeholder and line 314's setdefault("OPENAI_API_KEY","ollama") depend on ordering; also preserve the quirk that ollama-with-real-key still gets OPENAI_API_KEY="ollama" (real key goes only to OLLAMA_API_KEY). 3) Preserve setdefault-vs-assign exactly: openai branch assigns OPENAI_BASE_URL but setdefaults OPENAI_API_BASE; ollama setdefaults all three synthesized vars. 4) Keep the outer `if api_key:` / `if endpoint:` empty-string guards. 5) Place the `**{p: ("OPENAI_API_KEY",) for p in OPENAI_COMPATIBLE_LOCAL_PROVIDERS}` spread before explicit entries (or assert disjointness) so explicit provider names win on any future collision, matching elif precedence. 6) Scope trim: implement the key lookup table, but skip the three single-use endpoint helpers + dispatch map â€” the endpoint chain has only 3 short, commented branches and reads fine inline; helper+map indirection there is over-abstraction. 7) Run runner-service/tests/test_local_model_config.py (and test_opencode.py) to confirm.

title: One canonical agent-HOME resolver instead of four copies of the _agent_users fallback
description: The 'resolve (home, uid, gid), falling back to the `_agent_users` cache when effective_user is None because of runAsRoot' logic exists four times: `_resolve_home` in runner_mcp_config.py:219-237 (the most complete version), `_hermes_home` in hermes.py:46-55, `_resolve_openclaw_home` in openclaw.py:65-73, and inline twice in opencode.py (317-324 in `_ensure_config_dir` and 354-365 in `_agent_env`). All four reach into the private `agent_user._agent_users` dict directly. Beyond the duplication, the rationale comment ('runAsRoot resolves effective_user to None but the per-agent HOME still exists') is only present on one copy, so readers of the others can't tell why the fallback exists.
sketch:
Move the canonical helper into agent_user.py as a public function:

def resolve_agent_home(agent_user: Optional[dict], agent_id: Optional[str]) -> tuple[Optional[str], Optional[int], Optional[int]]:
    """(home, uid, gid); falls back to the runtime cache when agent_user is
    None/incomplete â€” runAsRoot spawns pass effective_user=None but the
    per-agent HOME still exists."""

runner_mcp_config._resolve_home becomes `from agent_user import resolve_agent_home as _resolve_home` (keeping its import-cycle guard if needed); delete `_hermes_home` (callers use the shared one + a tiny `_hermes_config_paths`), delete `_resolve_openclaw_home`, and replace the two opencode inline blocks. The private `_agent_users` import disappears from three backend files.
impact: ~35 lines removed across 4 files; the runAsRoot-fallback subtlety is documented exactly once; backends stop importing a private cache
guidance: 1) CRITICAL TRAP â€” opencode.py _agent_env: the config write at opencode.py:397-398 deliberately takes uid/gid from the ORIGINAL agent_user parameter, not the fallback-resolved dict (comment at 394-396: under runAsRoot, chowning the file to the agent UID locks it away from the root spawn that reads it back). The replacement there must be `home, _, _ = resolve_agent_home(agent_user, agent_id)` with lines 397-398 left untouched â€” do NOT thread the resolved uid/gid into _write_opencode_config_file. By contrast _ensure_config_dir (opencode.py:317-337) DOES intentionally chown with the fallback uid/gid, so there the full `home, uid, gid = resolve_agent_home(...)` is correct. The two opencode blocks are interchangeable on home resolution only. 2) Canonical semantics: use the dict-replacement style (hermes/openclaw/opencode) â€” on cache miss return (None, None, None). _resolve_home's current behavior differs only in returning stale uid/gid alongside home=None; every caller guards `if not home` first, so this is safe â€” but keep all those guards, don't 'simplify' them. 3) runner_instructions_config.py:47 imports _resolve_home and _atomic_write from runner_mcp_config â€” keep the alias re-export (`from agent_user import resolve_agent_home as _resolve_home`) in runner_mcp_config or repoint that import to agent_user. 4) The lazy try/except import inside _resolve_home (runner_mcp_config.py:228-232) is a defensive guard, not a real cycle â€” agent_user.py imports only config; drop it once the helper lives in agent_user.py. 5) hermes.py keeps its ensure_agent_user import (used elsewhere); only the _agent_users name is removed from the three backends. 6) Bonus fifth site the sketch missed: aider.py:103-113 _instructions_read_path does a cache-only home lookup, absorbable as `home, _, _ = resolve_agent_home(None, agent_id)` â€” same semantics since its agent_user is implicitly None.

title: Dedupe the GitHub-token env mirror between CliBackend._agent_env and claude_token_store.get_agent_env
description: The block that reads ~/.git-credentials and mirrors the token into GITHUB_TOKEN/GH_TOKEN/GITHUB_USER (+ GH_HOST/GITHUB_API_URL for GHES) is copy-pasted: cli_backend.py:322-335 and claude_token_store.py:858-876 are the same ~16 lines, including the identical 'x-access-token' special case and the github.com host check, with the claude copy wrapped in an extra try/except around the import. Both even carry cross-referencing comments ('same rationale as in CliBackend._agent_env'). The next GHES or token-format tweak must be made twice or the two CLI families diverge silently.
impact: ~30 duplicated lines â†’ 12 shared; GHES handling becomes single-site
guidance: Two corrections to the sketch. (1) The copies differ in error handling: claude_token_store.py:863-867 wraps import+call in try/except Exception (gh=None); cli_backend.py:323-324 does not. read_github_token_from_credentials only catches OSError internally (agent_user.py:568), so a .git-credentials file with invalid UTF-8 raises UnicodeDecodeError â€” swallowed today on the claude path, propagated (spawn crash) on the cli path. The sketched helper drops the try/except, silently changing the claude path. Either keep `except Exception: return` in the shared helper (unifies on swallow â€” arguably a fix for the cli path's crash, but make it a deliberate choice) or change the inner catch in read_github_token_from_credentials to `except (OSError, UnicodeDecodeError)`. (2) agent_user can be None at the cli_backend call site (tests call _agent_env(None, agent_id) â€” test_opencode.py:61,77,102); the sketched `agent_user.get("home")` would AttributeError. Use `agent_user.get("home") if agent_user else None` or keep the existing truthiness guard. Minor: keep the deferred function-level `from agent_user import ...` at both call sites (existing copies do this, likely circular-import avoidance), and move the rationale comment into the helper docstring so it lives once.

title: Collapse PtySession's twin file-watch loops (creds vs files) into one generic watcher
description: PtySession runs two structurally identical polling subsystems: the single-file creds watcher (`_capture_creds_baseline` 531-542, `_creds_sync_loop` 544-563, `_maybe_sync_creds` 565-609) and the N-file config watcher (`_read_watched_files`/`_files_signature`/`_files_max_mtime`/`_capture_files_baseline`/`_files_sync_loop`/`_maybe_sync_files`, 611-691). Both implement: capture baseline at start â†’ sleep CREDS_SYNC_INTERVAL_SEC loop â†’ mtime-gate â†’ read â†’ content-dedup (dedup_key vs sha256 signature) â†’ run_in_executor(on_change) â†’ advance markers, with mirrored wiring in `start()` (518-523) and `close()` (1331-1336) and six dedicated dataclass fields (261-266). The comment at 611 even labels it 'parallel to the single-file creds one above'. The loops' subtle invariants (raise-on-persist-failure â‡’ don't advance marker; advance mtime on unparseable content) are documented on one twin and not the other.
sketch:
Extract a small class in the same module:

class _FileWatcher:
    def __init__(self, paths: list[str], read: Callable[[], Optional[T]],
                 dedup_key: Callable[[T], Optional[str]], on_change: Callable[[T], None], label: str): ...
    def capture_baseline(self) -> None: ...
    async def run(self, closed: Callable[[], bool]) -> None: ...   # the shared loop
    async def poll_once(self) -> None: ...                          # called by close() for the final sync

PtySession keeps `creds_watch_path/creds_on_change/creds_dedup_key/files_watch_paths/files_on_change` as the recipe surface (no contract change) but builds at most two _FileWatcher instances in start(): the creds one with read=json-load + dedup_key passthrough, the files one with read=_read_watched_files + dedup_key=sha256-signature. close() calls `await creds_watcher.poll_once()` for the last-moment /login sync. Six fields and two loop bodies become one mechanism with the invariants documented once.
impact: ~140 lines â†’ ~85; one loop body to reason about instead of two with diverging comments
guidance: The twins are NOT byte-identical in semantics; the description slightly misstates one divergence and the implementer must encode all of these in the generic contract rather than "unifying" them away: (1) Read-failure policy DIFFERS: creds with unparseable/unreadable JSON does NOT advance the mtime marker (pty_session.py:577-579, retries next tick), while creds with parseable-JSON-but-no-token DOES advance (589-593), and files with zero readable files DOES advance (672-675). Contract: read() returning None => no marker advance (retry); read() succeeding but dedup_key returning falsy => advance mtime and skip. Files' read must return {} (never None) and its dedup_key must map empty->None. (2) creds_dedup_key is OPTIONAL with an mtime-only fallback (582-596, documented at 219-224); also a dedup_key that RAISES is treated as no-token and advances mtime (584-593) â€” keep both. After a successful on_change, the key marker advances only `if new_key is not None` (603-604); preserve. (3) Baseline atomicity: in _capture_creds_baseline one try covers both statements, so a json.load failure resets BOTH mtime->0.0 AND key->None (531-542); the generic capture_baseline must keep that coupling. (4) close() asymmetry and ordering: the final sync runs only for creds, BEFORE _closed=True and before task cancellation (1317-1336); files gets no final sync â€” the sketch preserves this, keep it. Also construct the watcher objects eagerly (e.g. __post_init__), not inside start(): close() can run on spawn-failure paths before start() completes, and today the final creds sync still works because _maybe_sync_creds reads constructor fields; a watcher built only in start() would silently skip it. Keep baseline capture in start() after seed_credentials_file ordering (comment 513-517). (5) Keep distinct success log lines (606-609 vs 688-691) via a label/format param â€” the creds one ("triggered by in-TUI login") is operationally meaningful. (6) Update the cross-file comments naming the old methods: backends/claude_code.py:183 and backends/hermes.py:184. (7) No tests pin this subsystem, so add nothing behavior-changing "while in there"; the loop interval (CREDS_SYNC_INTERVAL_SEC for both) and two-independent-tasks structure must stay as-is.

title: Table-drive the startup-prompt auto-answers and share the trust/bypass patterns between the two PTY drivers
description: Two related problems. (1) `PtySession._maybe_auto_answer_startup_prompt` (pty_session.py:969-1035) is four sequential copy-pasted blocks â€” codex-update, opencode-update, trust, bypass â€” each repeating the same 'not in _auto_answered â†’ regex.search(tail) â†’ log â†’ keystrokes â†’ add key â†’ stamp cooldown' choreography, including a `confirm_after_render_tick` inner coroutine defined twice verbatim (1005-1009 and 1030-1034). Adding the next CLI's update prompt means pasting a fifth block. (2) The trust-folder and bypass-permissions knowledge is duplicated across drivers: `_TRUST_RE`/`_BYPASS_PERMS_RE` exist in pty_session.py:115-126 AND claude_interactive.py:228-243 with slightly different alternations, and the answer recipes ('trust â†’ bare Enter because option 1 is highlighted', 'bypass â†’ Down, pause ~0.12s, Enter') are re-derived independently at pty_session.py:1013-1035 and claude_interactive.py:692-722. A Claude Code TUI wording change must be chased through two regexes and two answer implementations.
sketch:
New small module runner-service/src/backends/startup_prompts.py:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class StartupPrompt:
    key: str            # "trust", "bypass", "codex_update", "opencode_update"
    pattern: re.Pattern
    keys: tuple[bytes, ...]        # e.g. (b"\x1b[B", PAUSE, b"\r")
    log: str

STARTUP_PROMPTS: tuple[StartupPrompt, ...] = (...)

pty_session: _maybe_auto_answer_startup_prompt becomes one loop over STARTUP_PROMPTS:
    for p in STARTUP_PROMPTS:
        if p.key in self._auto_answered or not p.pattern.search(tail): continue
        self._send_keys(p.keys)   # one helper that handles the render-tick pause
        self._auto_answered.add(p.key); self._last_auto_answer_at = now; return

claude_interactive imports the same _TRUST_RE/_BYPASS_PERMS_RE constants (its Y/N + arrow-selector + fallback-LLM branches stay as-is since they need the resolver).
impact: pty_session's 67-line answer function becomes ~25 + a declarative table; the trust/bypass TUI knowledge exists once instead of in four places
guidance: 1) Sync-context trap: test_auto_answers_codex_update_prompt (tests/test_pty_session.py:54-70) runs with NO event loop; a _send_keys helper that always uses asyncio.create_task will crash with "no running event loop". Send pause-free recipes synchronously inline; only create_task when the recipe contains a PAUSE sentinel. 2) Route every write (including the deferred Enter) through the bound self._write_keystroke â€” tests monkeypatch it (lines 24, 42, 59, 79) and it re-checks _closed/master_fd at deferred-fire time. 3) Stamp _last_auto_answer_at once at fire time; the deferred \r must NOT re-stamp the cooldown. Preserve table order codex_update, opencode_update, trust, bypass (match priority). Keep the deferred sleep exactly asyncio.sleep(0.12), no jitter. 4) Do NOT place the shared module under backends/ as sketched: backends/__init__.py:43 instantiates the BACKEND singleton (and raises on unknown RUNNER_TYPE) at package import, and pty_session currently imports nothing from backends. Use a leaf src/startup_prompts.py importing only re/dataclasses. 5) _TRUST_RE is not identical across files: pty_session.py:117 has an extra alternative ("do you trust the contents of this director(y|ies)") missing from claude_interactive.py:228-234. Importing "the same _TRUST_RE" into claude_interactive widens its matching (its trust branch at line 692 preempts the numbered-choice/arrow/fallback-LLM branches) â€” not strictly behavior-preserving. Share _BYPASS_PERMS_RE verbatim; build the trust pattern from a shared alternation list with pty_session appending its extra wording, or consciously accept and document the widening. 6) Temper the impact claim: answer recipes stay duplicated by necessity (claude_interactive uses blocking _human_pause with Â±50% jitter in a thread executor, _ship_keystroke str+auto-\r, returns "1"/"2", bumps nonlocal stream_raw_offset/detection_offset); only the regexes and pty_session's recipes consolidate.

title: Extract SSE chunk builders in routes_api.py to flatten the three streaming generators
description: routes_api.py has three async SSE generators â€” `event_generator` (575-598), `stream_openai_response` (663-741), `stream_openai_completion_response` (796-844) â€” whose bodies are dominated by 200+-char single-line yields of the form `yield f"data: {json.dumps({'id': completion_id, 'object': 'chat.completion.chunk', 'created': created, 'model': model, 'choices': [{'index': 0, 'delta': {...}, 'finish_reason': None}]})}\n\n"` repeated 7 times in stream_openai_response alone with only the delta payload differing (lines 667, 692, 695, 700, 710, 714, 718, 726). The usage/finish-chunk construction is also duplicated between the streaming and non-streaming paths of both OpenAI endpoints (728-740 vs 759-765, and 832-843 vs 858-863), each re-deriving `total_tokens or (input + output)` independently. The event-type dispatch logic is impossible to skim because every branch is a wall of serialization.
impact: ~60 lines shorter and each generator's event dispatch becomes readable at a glance; the token-totals fallback formula exists once instead of four times
guidance: Three corrections to the sketch. (1) ensure_ascii is inconsistent across the existing yields: in stream_openai_response only the line-726 error yield uses ensure_ascii=False â€” lines 667, 692, 695, 700, 710, 714, 718, 740 use the default (escaped) form; event_generator uses ensure_ascii=False everywhere except line 577; the completions generator uses the default except line 830. A single _sse(obj) with ensure_ascii=False baked in changes wire bytes for non-ASCII content on most chat chunks. All known consumers JSON-parse so this is semantically safe, but either (a) accept and call out the normalization explicitly, or (b) give _sse an ensure_ascii parameter and preserve each call site's current flag. (2) The sketch's _usage_block with **({'runner_session_id': session_id} if session_id is not None else {}) is wrong: the chat finish chunk (line 737) and non-streaming chat usage (line 764) ALWAYS include runner_session_id, possibly null, while the completions usages (836-841, 858-863) NEVER include it. Use an omit-sentinel or include_session flag â€” chat paths always emit the key, completions paths never do; do not key on None. (3) _chat_chunk must conditionally include the 'usage' key (only the finish chunk has it) â€” never emit 'usage': null on intermediate chunks. Also: event_generator (575-598) emits a custom {'status','output'} schema, so only _sse applies there, and the completions endpoint needs a separate _completion_chunk ({'text': ..., 'finish_reason': ...}), not the chat delta builder. In the non-streaming usage blocks, keep passing the raw result.get('input_tokens', 0) expressions and apply the identical 'or' fallbacks in the helper â€” result.get can return None and the current code lets it propagate; do not add None-coercion while refactoring. Note there is zero test coverage of these three generators, so verify by diffing captured SSE output before/after against a stubbed BACKEND.
